skill.name: etetoolkit
skill.description: Phylogenetic tree analysis skill for ETE Toolkit workflows, including Newick tree manipulation, evolutionary event analysis, NCBI taxonomy lookup, and publication-ready visualization.
skill.summary: Analyze, transform, compare, and visualize phylogenetic trees with ETE Toolkit guidance and helper scripts.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most alerts are false positives from Markdown backticks, scientific terminology, and documentation examples. Confirmed risks are legitimate but user-visible: sudo setup commands, local file reads and writes, and ETE taxonomy database downloads. No evidence found of prompt injection, malicious intent, credential access, persistence, or data exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Package Installation Instructions
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes sudo apt-get commands for optional Qt rendering dependencies. This is a legitimate setup instruction, but an agent should not run privileged commands without explicit user approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo commands are present at the cited lines and require elevated privileges. The context is installation documentation, so the risk is controlled but real if executed automatically.

medium_findings.title: NCBI Taxonomy Auto-Download and Hidden Local Database
medium_findings.description: The ETE NCBITaxa workflow is documented as downloading about 300 MB of taxonomy data into a hidden home directory database. This creates network and filesystem side effects that users should approve before first use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited documentation explicitly describes the hidden database path and automatic download behavior. This appears to be standard ETE behavior, not malicious exfiltration.

medium_findings.title: Helper Scripts Read and Write User-Supplied File Paths
medium_findings.description: The included helper scripts load input tree files and write converted, pruned, rerooted, or rendered outputs. This is expected for the skill, but users should review paths to avoid overwriting important files.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code clearly reads and writes paths supplied by command-line arguments or workflow variables. No evidence found of destructive deletion, credential access, or unauthorized traversal.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Ruby Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command findings are false positives from Markdown code fences, inline code formatting, and legitimate Python examples. No evidence found that these backticks execute commands inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The representative locations are Markdown documentation blocks or inline API examples. They are not Ruby source files and do not provide a shell execution path.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Semantic False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts point to phylogenetics text, tree support values, and visualization examples. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or password hashing logic in the inspected files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found no cryptographic primitives at the cited representative locations. The static labels appear to match unrelated scientific terms or formatting.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are Benign Tree Statistics
low_findings.description: System reconnaissance alerts correspond to tree metadata, batch processing, or documentation examples. No evidence found of host enumeration, environment discovery, credential collection, or persistence behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines describe visualization features or enumerate input tree files in a user-selected directory. This is normal batch processing for phylogenetic data.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sudo Command in Setup Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: Privileged package installation appears in documentation and should require explicit user approval before execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The privileged commands are explicit and easy to verify. They are setup examples, not hidden execution code.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automatic Taxonomy Database Download
dangerous_patterns.description: NCBITaxa first use can download a large remote database and write it under the user home directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The behavior is documented directly in the cited lines and is a known ETE feature.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Output Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: Helper scripts accept output paths and render or write files to those paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file write behavior is direct and intentional. The risk depends on user-supplied destinations.

content.value_statement: Phylogenetic tree work often requires precise transformations, event analysis, taxonomy lookup, and visual outputs. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through ETE Toolkit workflows with focused examples and helper scripts.
actual_capabilities: Load and manipulate Newick, NHX, PhyloXML, and NeXML tree formats with ETE patterns.
Reroot, prune, traverse, compare, and summarize phylogenetic trees.
Guide gene tree workflows for duplication, speciation, orthology, and paralogy analysis.
Use NCBI taxonomy examples for lineage lookup, taxonomic annotation, and topology work.
Create PDF, SVG, or PNG tree visualizations with ETE styling guidance.
Run included helper scripts for tree operations and quick visualization when dependencies are installed.
limitations: Requires a working Python environment with ete3 installed.
Rendering features may require Qt or PyQt system dependencies.
NCBI taxonomy workflows can download a large local database on first use.
The skill does not validate biological correctness of input trees or alignments.

use_cases.title: Prepare trees for comparative analysis
use_cases.description: Clean, reroot, prune, and convert phylogenetic trees before downstream research workflows.

use_cases.title: Investigate gene family evolution
use_cases.description: Identify duplication and speciation events, then organize ortholog and paralog groups for review.

use_cases.title: Generate publication figures
use_cases.description: Style tree layouts, labels, support values, and exports for reports, papers, or presentations.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize a Newick tree
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the etetoolkit skill to inspect my Newick tree file. Report leaf count, total nodes, branch length summary, and any parsing assumptions.

prompt_templates.title: Reroot and prune taxa
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the etetoolkit workflow to reroot this tree at the midpoint, prune it to my taxa list, and explain each output file created.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze gene tree events
prompt_templates.prompt: Use ETE PhyloTree guidance to detect duplication and speciation events in this gene tree. Summarize likely ortholog groups and caveats.

prompt_templates.title: Build a styled tree figure
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an ETE visualization plan for this phylogenetic tree with support labels, colored clades, and SVG and PDF outputs for publication.

output_examples.input: A user provides a Newick file and asks for basic tree statistics.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports leaf count, node count, depth, branch length summary, support value summary, and parsing notes.

output_examples.input: A user asks to compare several phylogenetic trees.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes Robinson-Foulds distances, highlights topology differences, and recommends follow-up checks for incompatible taxa.

output_examples.input: A user needs a figure-ready circular tree.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes layout settings, label options, support coloring, export formats, and dependency checks for rendering.

best_practices: Confirm tree format numbers and taxon naming rules before running transformations.
Use explicit output paths and keep original tree files unchanged.
Ask before installing system packages or downloading the NCBI taxonomy database.
anti_patterns: Running sudo installation commands without explicit user approval.
Overwriting source tree files during conversion or rendering.
Interpreting orthology results without checking species naming and tree quality.

faq.question: What is ETE Toolkit used for?
faq.answer: ETE Toolkit is used for phylogenetic tree manipulation, evolutionary analysis, taxonomy integration, and tree visualization.

faq.question: Does this skill include executable scripts?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes helper scripts for tree operations and quick visualization. Review input and output paths before running them.

faq.question: Can it create publication figures?
faq.answer: Yes. The visualization guidance covers ETE styling and exports such as PDF, SVG, and PNG.

faq.question: Does it require internet access?
faq.answer: Most tree operations do not. NCBI taxonomy setup or updates can download a large local database.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill has legitimate filesystem operations, setup commands, and optional database downloads. No malicious behavior was found.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
